skill.name: conflict-of-interest-checker
skill.description: Check for co-authorship and institutional conflicts between authors and suggested reviewers to support peer review integrity. Coauthorship and institutional conflict detection supported.
skill.summary: Automated conflict-of-interest detection for peer review, checking co-authorship and institutional conflicts between authors and suggested reviewers.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 51 static analysis findings were evaluated and dismissed as false positives. The 15 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings target hex hash values in JSON audit data, score numbers in changelogs, and plain markdown text. The 34 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code block delimiters in .md files, not executable Ruby code. The 4 'System reconnaissance' findings are test output data and CSV format examples. The 2 'Hardcoded URL' findings are the skill's source attribution link. The Python script uses only standard library modules (argparse, csv, sys, collections) with no network calls, no eval/exec, no credential access, and no obfuscation. The skill is a legitimate academic peer-review tool.

content.user_title: Detect Conflicts of Interest in Peer Review
content.value_statement: Manually checking for co-authorship and institutional conflicts between authors and reviewers is slow and error-prone. This skill automates conflict detection by comparing publication records and affiliations to produce clear reports with recommendations.
actual_capabilities: Detect co-authorship conflicts by comparing shared publication records between authors and reviewers
Identify institutional conflicts when authors and reviewers belong to the same institution
Generate structured conflict reports with Accept or Recuse recommendations for each reviewer
Load custom CSV publication files for real-world journal submission data
Support both automated script execution and direct AI reasoning for conflict detection
Flag conflicts with detailed shared publication information
limitations: Collaboration window checking for recent co-authors is not implemented
Does not connect to external publication databases or search engines
Requires explicit author and reviewer name lists to function
Institutional conflict detection needs reviewer institution name as input

use_cases.title: Journal Submission Review
use_cases.description: Check for conflicts between manuscript authors and proposed reviewers before submitting to a journal.

use_cases.title: Editorial Conflict Screening
use_cases.description: Screen suggested reviewers for co-authorship or institutional ties during editorial decision-making.

use_cases.title: Grant Review Panel Compliance
use_cases.description: Verify that grant proposal reviewers have no undisclosed conflicts with applicants.
use_cases.target_user: Grant administrators and funding panel coordinators

prompt_templates.title: Basic Co-Authorship Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check for co-authorship conflicts between authors Smith, Jones, Lee and reviewers Brown, Davis, Wilson.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick conflict check for a journal submission with 3 authors and 3 reviewers.

prompt_templates.title: Conflict Check with Publication Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a conflict check using the file pubs.csv. Authors are Smith and Jones. Reviewers are Brown and Davis. The CSV has columns author, reviewer, and paper_id.
prompt_templates.scenario: Using a custom CSV publication file for conflict detection.

prompt_templates.title: Institutional Conflict Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check for institutional conflicts. Authors are Smith from Harvard and Jones from Stanford. Reviewer is Brown from Harvard. Also check co-authorship conflicts using the publications file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Combined institutional and co-authorship conflict detection.

prompt_templates.title: Full Manuscript Review Decision Support
prompt_templates.prompt: For manuscript MS-2024-001, authors are Smith (Harvard), Jones (MIT), Lee (Stanford). Proposed reviewers are Brown (Harvard), Davis (Stanford), Wilson (MIT). Check all conflict types and provide a structured report with recommendations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive conflict check for editorial decision-making with institutional and co-authorship analysis.

output_examples.input: Check conflicts for authors Smith, Jones and reviewers Brown, Davis
Check conflicts for authors Taylor, Adams and reviewers White, Black

best_practices: Provide the complete list of author and reviewer names to ensure all potential conflicts are detected
Use a CSV file with accurate publication records to improve detection beyond demo data
Always manually review flagged conflicts before making final editorial decisions
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to evaluate manuscript quality, write peer reviews, or assess research validity
Do not assume that an absence of detected conflicts guarantees reviewer impartiality
Do not provide incomplete or guessed name lists, as this will miss potential conflicts

faq.question: What types of conflicts can this skill detect?
faq.answer: It detects co-authorship conflicts through shared publication records and institutional conflicts when authors and reviewers belong to the same institution.

faq.question: How do I prepare a publication CSV file?
faq.answer: Create a CSV with at least two columns: author for author name and paper_id for unique paper identifier. An optional reviewer column can also be used.

faq.question: Can this skill search external databases?
faq.answer: No. It only uses the data you provide through command-line arguments or a CSV file. It does not connect to any external publication databases.

faq.question: What if I only have author and reviewer names?
faq.answer: The skill includes demo publication data for testing. For accurate results, provide a CSV file with actual publication records.

faq.question: How should I use the conflict report?
faq.answer: Use the report to identify flagged reviewers. Exclude them from consideration and document the reasons for exclusion. Select alternative reviewers as needed.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for grant review panels?
faq.answer: Yes. It can screen grant proposal reviewers for co-authorship and institutional conflicts with applicants, supporting compliance with conflict-of-interest policies.
